agent_raw:
## Assessment of Streaming Platform Offline Recommendation Benchmark

The experiment compares two full recommendation stacks — legacy (matrix factorization retriever + GBDT reranker, 200 candidates) vs. new (two-tower retriever + transformer reranker, 500 candidates) — on a replay NDCG@20 metric. The new stack wins by 4.5% relative. The team wants to interpret this as evidence that the new ranking model is stronger.

The design has a critical structural flaw that prevents this interpretation, plus several secondary measurement concerns.

### Primary Issue: The Experiment Cannot Attribute the Gain to the Ranking Model

Three things changed at once: the retrieval model, the reranker, and the candidate pool size. The NDCG@20 comparison is further compromised because each reranker is scored only on its own retriever's output — the systems are never evaluated on a shared candidate set. This means the metric difference absorbs contributions from all three changes simultaneously.

NDCG@20 is conditioned on the candidate set. Handing the transformer reranker 500 candidates and the GBDT 200 candidates creates a structural asymmetry: the 500-item pool will contain more relevant items in expectation, giving the new reranker a higher relevance ceiling before it has ranked anything. A 4.5% NDCG@20 improvement could be fully explained by the retrieval recall improvement and pool-size expansion alone, without any improvement in ranking model quality.

### The Required Analysis: 2x2 Factorial Ablation

The minimum analysis required to support the leadership claim is a 2x2 factorial:
- Rows: legacy MF retriever (200-item pool) vs. two-tower retriever (500-item pool)
- Columns: GBDT reranker vs. transformer reranker

This gives four NDCG@20 measurements. The cell (transformer reranker, MF 200-item pool) directly tests whether the ranker adds value independent of the retrieval improvement. The cell (GBDT reranker, two-tower 500-item pool) tests the pool-size and retriever contributions without changing the ranker.

This ablation is feasible post-hoc on the existing replay dataset and does not require production redeployment. Its absence is the primary gap in the current analysis.

### Secondary Issues

**Replay evaluation biases:** The replay metric has two biases that both work against the new system: (a) novel two-tower candidates that were never historically impressed receive zero relevance credit, undercounting the new retriever's contribution; (b) click-based relevance labels reflect historical position-dependent exposure from the legacy ranking policy, creating an implicit preference for systems that reproduce the legacy ordering. Both biases make the 4.5% lift conservative — the direction of the finding is robust, but the magnitude is not a clean quality estimate.

**User sample:** The 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out split condition the evaluation on active, recent users. Cold-start and low-frequency users are underrepresented. This is a limitation inherent to offline replay evaluation. Stratified results by user activity tier would help assess generalizability.

### What Leadership Should Be Told

The experiment shows the new end-to-end stack outperforms the legacy end-to-end stack by 4.5% relative NDCG@20 on this evaluation protocol. This is a real signal. It cannot be used as evidence that the new ranking model is stronger without running the 2x2 factorial ablation first. The ablation is feasible on existing data and should be completed before the result is presented as a ranking model endorsement.
all_issues_raised: Primary: Three simultaneous changes (retrieval model, reranker, pool size) folded into one NDCG@20 number with no ablation to decompose them
Primary: Each reranker scored on its own retriever's output — different conditioning sets make the comparison invalid as a ranker test
Primary: Factorial ablation absent despite feasibility on existing offline data
Secondary: Replay evaluation missing-data bias — novel two-tower candidates receive zero credit (bias against new system)
Secondary: Position bias in click labels encodes legacy ranker's exposure decisions (bias against new system)
Secondary: 30-day log expiry and leave-last-week-out select for active users (inherent offline evaluation limitation)
